era_context: The early 15th century is characterized by the Ming Dynasty's consolidation under Emperor Yongle, the Ottoman Empire's expansion in the Balkans and Anatolia, and the Great Schism in the Catholic Church. In Europe, the Hundred Years' War continues between England and France, with factional tensions emerging in France. The Portuguese Age of Exploration is beginning under Prince Henry the Navigator. In Asia, Zheng He's treasure voyages are underway, and the Ming Dynasty is at the height of its naval and diplomatic power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia and the Pacific (sparse European records; Chinese sources may be underrepresented)
Indigenous Americas (reliance on post-conquest accounts and archaeology)

events.title: Zheng He's first treasure voyage continues through Southeast Asia
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's first great treasure voyage, which departed from Nanjing in 1405, continues through Southeast Asia in 1406. The massive fleet of over 300 ships and 27,000 men visits ports in Java, Sumatra, and other Southeast Asian states, establishing tributary relationships and collecting exotic goods for the Ming court. The voyage demonstrates Ming naval supremacy and diplomatic prestige. The fleet's size and organization are unprecedented in world history at this time, far exceeding contemporary European naval capabilities.
key_figures: Southeast Asian rulers and tributary states

sources.name: Luo Maodeng, Longjiang Baochuan (later compilation, 16th century)
Archaeological evidence from shipwrecks and port sites

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's first voyage is well-documented in Ming imperial records. The voyage's continuation through Southeast Asia in 1406 is established in contemporary sources.

events.title: Ming Dynasty consolidates control over Annam (Vietnam)
events.description: Emperor Yongle of the Ming Dynasty completes the military conquest and consolidation of Annam (northern Vietnam), which had been partially independent under the Later Trần Dynasty. The Ming establish direct administrative control, integrate the region into the imperial bureaucracy, and suppress local resistance. This consolidation expands Ming territorial control and secures the southern frontier. However, the occupation is unpopular and will eventually lead to resistance movements and the restoration of Vietnamese independence under the Lê Dynasty (1427).
events.certainty_note: Ming consolidation of Annam is documented in imperial records, but the specific date of 1406 is approximate. The process occurred over several years, with major campaigns in 1407–1427.
cross_references: Part of broader Ming territorial expansion (1402–1424)
Leads to Vietnamese resistance and independence (1427)

events.title: English-French negotiations and truces in the Hundred Years' War
events.description: Following the Treaty of Paris (1396), which established a 28-year truce between England and France, negotiations and periodic truces continue in 1406. However, the truce is fragile, and both sides continue military preparations and raids. The assassination of Louis of Orléans in 1407 will destabilize France further and eventually lead to English military intervention under Henry V. In 1406, the situation remains relatively stable, but tensions are rising.

sources.name: English royal records and parliamentary rolls

events.certainty_note: English-French negotiations are documented in royal records, but specific events in 1406 are less precisely dated. The general trajectory of the truce and its fragility are confirmed.
cross_references: Leads to 1407-001 (assassination of Louis of Orléans)

events.title: Portuguese exploration of the African coast continues under Prince Henry
events.description: Under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator, Portuguese expeditions continue to explore and map the West African coast. By 1406, Portuguese ships have established trading posts and forts along the Moroccan and Mauritanian coasts, seeking gold, spices, and enslaved people. These early voyages lay the groundwork for the Portuguese Age of Exploration and the eventual rounding of the Cape of Good Hope. The expeditions are motivated by economic gain, religious conversion, and geographic curiosity.

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery and Conquest of Guinea (15th-century compilation)
Portuguese royal records and trading post documentation

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of the African coast is well-documented, but specific events in 1406 are less precisely dated. The general trajectory of exploration is confirmed; individual voyage dates may be approximate.
cross_references: Part of broader Portuguese Age of Exploration (1415–1500s)

disconfirming_evidence: The Ming consolidation of Annam is sometimes portrayed as a straightforward conquest and integration, but modern scholarship emphasizes that it was unpopular and eventually led to sustained resistance. The occupation lasted only until 1427, when the Lê Dynasty restored Vietnamese independence. The significance of the Ming consolidation should be understood as temporary rather than permanent. The 'Age of Exploration' framing applied to Portuguese activities in 1406 is somewhat anachronistic; these were primarily trading and raiding expeditions, not systematic exploration in the later sense.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1406 is moderate but geographically uneven. European events (English-French negotiations) are documented in royal records and chronicles, with a Western institutional bias. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) provide detailed records of Zheng He's voyages and Ming territorial consolidation. Vietnamese sources are later compilations, introducing potential dating uncertainties. Portuguese sources are later compilations, introducing potential dating uncertainties. African and indigenous American history remains poorly documented for this year, relying on later accounts and archaeology. The historiographic tradition privileges European and East Asian sources, potentially marginalizing other regions.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of first voyage through Southeast Asia in 1406 led to its continuation through the Indian Ocean in 1407
Ming consolidation of Annam in 1406 eventually led to Vietnamese resistance and independence in 1427
English-French negotiations in 1406 were destabilized by the assassination of Louis of Orléans in 1407
Portuguese exploration in 1406 is part of the broader Age of Exploration (15th–17th centuries)
